comments.title: Practical magic for Celtic GD Ovates
comments.content:
John Michael,

at the end of the Ovate chapter of your Celtic Golden Dawn book, you have written that:

"In either case, your rank and initiation as an Ovate remain with you, and you may freely use the teachings you have already received in whatever way may correspond to the requirements of your Ovate obligation and your own personal decisions and needs."

Greer, John Michael (2013-02-08). The Celtic Golden Dawn: An Original & Complete Curriculum of Druidical Study (Kindle Locations 2455-2457). Llewellyn Worldwide, LTD.. Kindle Edition.

Suppose a person was to work through all the Ovate Knowledge Lectures, doing everything as written, but they were not interested in ever proceeding to the Bardic and Druid Grades. Would any options of practical magic be open to them?

I do not mean mixing Celtic GD with another theurgic system. Rather, I was thinking about adding some tradition-agnostic techniques, such as:

- working towards astral projection using the exercises in Denning & Phillips' Practical Guide to Astral Projection,
- sympathetic magic for personality improvement and sending good luck to friends and family (with their permission, of course),
- pathworkings, such as those in The Initiate's Book of Pathworkings: A Bridge of Dreams by Dolores Ashcroft-Nowicki.

Would it be okay to perform these kinds of workings in an Ovate Temple?

replies.title: Re: Practical magic for Celtic GD Ovates
replies.content:
I don't recommend it. The point of encouraging people to figure out whether they want to stop at the Ovate Grade, or proceed to the next, is that the more complex magical practices aren't appropriate for everyone, and some will want to stop at the Ovate level and proceed with whatever their core interests happen to be. For example, an astrologer, a diviner, an herbalist, or an alchemist might find quite a bit of value in the Ovate material but choose not to go on to the Pathworkings and elemental invocations of the Bardic Grade, or the full-on ceremonial magic of the Druid Grade. The point isn't to encourage people to do a mashup of DOGD and other stuff.

If you want to do Pathworkings, in other words, why not do the ones in the Bardic Grade, the grade that follows Ovate? All the preparations you've made while studying the Ovate Grade would feed directly into that, where Dolores' Pathworkings use a completely different symbolism and you'd more or less have to start over again from scratch. That's true more generally -- the things you've cited aren't actually "tradition-agnostic" at all; the first and third derive from specific traditions (the Ogdoadic tradition of the Aurum Solis and the lineage that runs from the Golden Dawn through Dion Fortune's Society of the Inner Light to Dolores' Servants of the Light, respectively), while the second would depend on which particular system of sympathetic magic you happen to be using. You could instead keep on working with the DOGD system, in which you've already invested serious time and effort. Why does that not seem like a good idea to you?

replies.title: Re: Practical magic for Celtic GD Ovates
replies.content:
Well, I guess there are three reasons why I wanted to do this:

1. visualizing a center of energy at the feet seems a little weird to me,
2. I do not know how much time per day or week would working with the Bardic and Druid grades entail,
3. I wanted to do practical magic sooner than the Druid grade.

The first reason and third reasons I can live with.

As for the second, do you have any estimates as to the minimum practice times required daily and weekly for the different grades? 30 minutes a day is about the limit of what I'm willing to invest, with a longer working once per week or so.

(BTW, thanks for the detailed answer so far!)

replies.title: Re: Practical magic for Celtic GD Ovates
replies.content:
How much you get out of magic will always be determined by how much you put into it. If you're willing to invest thirty minutes a day -- which is more than a lot of people will do! -- the results you get, no matter what system you practice, will be thirty minutes a day worth of magic. That said, you can complete the entire system of magic taught in The Celtic Golden Dawn on thirty minutes a day of regular practices, with some extra time for longer workings once each week or so, and some time for study and reading -- how long depends entirely on how fast you read, and absorb what you read.

BTW, why do you find an energy center at your feet weird? Did you think your energy body doesn't include your feet, or contact the earth energies at the lowest point of your body?

replies.title: Re: Practical magic for Celtic GD Ovates
replies.content: I guess I find the feet center weird because a single current of energy is supposed to pass through two legs. My mind would find it more logical if the light split at the genital center, either creating two smaller earth centers, one at each foot, or rejoining and creating a single large center covering both feet.

replies.title: Re: Practical magic for Celtic GD Ovates
replies.content: Ah, I see the problem. The current of light moves through the space occupied by your physical body, but it isn't in your physical body -- it's in your energetic body, which is an ovoid extending two or three feet out from your physical body. Your Earth center is at the lowest pole of your energetic body no matter where your legs happen to be.

comments.title: Apology
comments.content:
Hello JMG,
This doesn’t need to be posted; it’s your call: First, I think I owe you an explanation and/or apology from last week, when I enquired about 4-letter deity names from the Greek Pantheon. You seemed irritated, if not offended, that I was wasting your time by not reading you closely enough and not doing my own homework. I felt stung by your response, and I hope I might undo any offense.

First, I should have been conscious that you, of all people, use language with precision; please understand that my life as a father/husband/doctor has habituated me to parsing the imprecise use of language by others, and I frequently find myself seeking to confirm what I think I heard/read. I did that with you. I’m sorry.

Second, when you suggested Hades, Tyche, Eros, Aeon, …, I realized immediately that by “Pantheon”, I had been implicitly assuming the term referred only to the Olympian gods, but (I slap my forehead) “pantheon” does mean all gods, no? So I went off and happily discovered Iaso, goddess of recovery, daughter of Aesclepius. There seems precious little extent material about her, but I feel drawn. Aeon seems pretty severe, but I would hope that such powers would be turned to good effect upon the negative influences that the LRP is intended, after all, to banish. So my deities for the 4 directions in the LRP are Iaso, Eros, Tyche and Aeon. Again, my apologies for any offense I have given. My Aspergers, which I appear to have, is probably more severe than yours.

replies.title: Re: Apology
replies.content:
I seem to have come across more crabbily than I intended, so please accept my apology in return. Yes, "pantheon" does mean "all the gods," and the Hermetic GD's version of the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram uses a really odd assortment of divine names, including one -- AGLA -- that isn't actually a name at all, but an acronym! So you can range just as far afield as you wish.

Your set of four names ought to do fine. I'd never heard of Iaso, either, but there she is, and one of the benefits of polytheism is that you really can adapt rites such as this one to fit your personal needs.

comments.title: Chance
comments.content: Do you believe in randomness? Do you think that the physical world has a random or chaotic element, that it just seems indeterminate to us? What role does chance or randomness play if any in occult/magical philosophy? How does this reconcile with the different planes of existence? I recall you mentioning Ilya Prigogine some time ago and I was wondering what your thoughts were on his work.

replies.title: Re: Chance
replies.content: We know from physics that the basis of material reality, the quantum level, is in fact random -- it doesn't just seem indeterminate to us, it actually is indeterminate and governed by random factors. The world as we know it is generated by the interplay of random events and patterns of natural order, which is why strict determinism (the notion that everything is preordained and every event in the universe could have been predicted in advance by a sufficiently powerful mind) is demonstrably nonsense. The different planes of existence all have their own comparable interplay of order and chaos -- these two are not opposed, as in cheap fantasy fiction, but complementary, the yang and yin of the unfolding cosmos. I think very highly of Prigogine's work, and if I ever get around to writing one of those big books of occult philosophy, it's going to draw on Prigogine, Stephen Wolfram, Charles Darwin, et al. as much as on occult writings.

replies.title: Re: Chance
replies.content:
I would read that.

Have you spent time playing with Conway's Game of Life and other simulations of dynamics and what role do you think this has for this type of understanding - can the same thing be achieved through meditation? Is the New Kind of Science similar to divination? Has anyone else you are aware of made the connection between complex systems science and occult philosophy?

replies.title: Re: Chance
replies.content: I'm not at all familiar with Conway's Game of Life, whatever that might be -- I've spent my time studying magic and occultism rather than complex systems science. ;-) Wolfram's A New Kind of Science is a theory of the generation of complexity, while divination is a practical technique -- you might as well ask whether the theory of gravity is similar to a pendulum clock. As for people noticing the connection between complex systems and occult philosophy, as I recall, chaos theory was borrowed to some degree by Peter Carroll and some of the other writers on chaos magic, but that's the one point of contact I know of.

replies.title: Re: Chance
replies.content:
Fair enough. I'm fishing for a connection there and thought that might spark something even if it was an inappropriate metaphor. I suppose by that I mean that Wolfram's theory says you cannot understand the system from observing its parts - that you have to play with it to find the emergent properties. And both show an inherent random aspect.

Why does divination work and does it have a relationship to self-organization and emergence based on the inherent randomness of the physical realm?

replies.title: Re: Chance
replies.content: Nobody knows why divination works. The theory that it might have something to do with self-organization and emergence is doubtless worth looking into.

replies.title: Re: Chance
replies.content:
Conway's Game of Life is a cellular automaton that, like Wolfram's rule 110, is "class 4" and Turing-complete, thus capable of generating complex behavior. Its grid and rules involve two dimensions instead of one, and the rules are just slightly more complicated. But like rule 110, each cell is either black or white at each iteration. (A cell's next state depends on its current state and the total number of black cells among its eight nearest neighbors.) It was studied (mostly by recreational mathematicians) beginning several decades before Wolfram's more systematic explorations, and the evolution or behavior of certain types of configurations is more entertaining to watch, being a changing 2D image instead of the stacking up of successive rows of 1D cells.

@Anon, Wolfram's Rule 30 cellular automaton rule (and other comparable "class 3" rules) has been used in computer applications to generate high-quality (that is, thoroughly tested to be free of known statistical biases) pseudo-random numbers. In theory one could use it for a variant of geomancy, starting with a number of even-odd or one-zero or yes-no choices generated by your preferred geomantic method, then running the algorithm (which of course is completely deterministic, as in geomancy) to produce a succession of binary bit results. But you'd need to invent a convention to group and convert the stream of individual bits the algorithm outputs into some meaningful set of symbols. It's not clear what the advantage would be over other methods.

If you used a class 4 automaton like Life or rule 110 instead, you'd have to adjust somehow for some patterns in the results occurring more frequently than others.

replies.title: Re: Chance
replies.content:
Is Ilya Prigogine responsible for the theory that complexity in organic life might be driven by the need to dissipate energy excess?

I ask this because I am following, sadly with interruptions needed to set my life in order, the Celtic Golden Dawn course. This doesn't mean I've stopped with magic studies.

The source for the statement in the first paragraph is important to me because I've read about it long ago, as a statement that was not the main focus of an article in a Brazilian science magazine long ago.

This is important because I see it as an answer to two questions. The first one is, why is chlorophyll green? Being this color, it reflects away the section of light spectrum where the Sun energy is strongest. Why would such waste have been selected? I think the waste is the point. Early lifeforms were possibly much less resilient and unstable; rising complexity to dissipate energy excess would be one of the strategies that solved this problem; chlorophyll being green and getting rid of energy in the green portion of the spectrum would be another.

This of course would look like it is only tangentially related to magic, and it perhaps would be forgivable as a question about an author that was cited, but it is very important. One of the points where I got stuck without a solution in meditations was "What drives the affinity of consciousness to the lessons that it needs to learn?" As in, humans stop being so when they learned all lessons that were available in human form. But how would this work?

I think now, because the reverse is also true, "as below, so above," that like in the strategy to get rid from the energy excess, the rising complexity in consciousness, and its affinity to the lessons that it needs to learn, could be driven by the need to get rid of the creative energy pressure from "The Source." I'm not sure what I'm trying to convey here is clear, I hope it is; often I have trouble to express complex thoughts in English, as a second language to me.

Sometimes I need to write on wood, but I use a different burning procedure.

replies.content:
What process did you go through to decide who would be a good source to learn from?

I did a search on Yronwode and in one article and its comments she is accused of some things (http://conjureart.blogspot.com/2013/10/open-letter-to-cat-yronwode-and-lucky.html) that if true would put her in opposition to some of your principles.

replies.title: Re: Yronwode
replies.content:
I'm not sure where people got the idea that a teacher of magic ought to be a plaster saint. If you'll read my comment above, you'll note that I never claimed that Cat was perfect, or that she'd never made mistakes, or that she'd never been accused of anything by anybody. I simply claimed that she was a competent teacher of Southern conjure -- as indeed she is.

In general, I have no patience with this currently fashionable notion that any value a person might have is utterly erased if somebody can dredge up some accusation of wrongdoing against them. No doubt if you look hard you can find somebody who's accused me of something or other, you know -- and no doubt you, too, have done something or other in your life that you're not too proud of just now. So?

To answer your question, I met Cat at a Golden Dawn event in northern California, where I was teaching a workshop on geomancy. We got to talking, I found her interesting and very well informed, and I signed up for her correspondence course. Since I choose my teachers on the basis of whether they know what they claim to be able to teach, rather than looking for a shining paragon of virtue about whom nobody has ever had a negative thought, that was sufficient for my needs.

replies.title: Re: Yronwode
replies.content:
Not pertinent to magic particularly, but rather to the "fashionable notion" to which you refer above:

https://www.yahoo.com/lifestyle/laura-ingalls-wilders-name-removed-award-racism-concerns-221528580.html

'Tis a sad thing.

David, by the lake

replies.title: Re: Yronwode
replies.content:
Yeah, I heard about that. It's a classic example of the same sort of thing.

I sometimes think that the frantic quest to find some way to find some justification or other to erase the existence of dead authors is motivated by jealousy. You rarely hear that kind of thing being pushed by people who actually know how to write; it's the also-rans, those whose writing will never be more than third-rate, who are busy trying to tear down the reputations of writers from the past. I suppose they think that if they just censor the past with enough brutality, people will be forced to read their work because there's nothing else available.

replies.title: Re: Yronwode
replies.content: How do you embed links in Dreamwidth? What I did there according to the instructions was supposed to provide a link to the article, not just bold the words and put the web address in brackets.

replies.title: Re: Yronwode
replies.content:
What our host just said!

Cat and I have crossed swords in a small way, too, in the past. Nonetheless, I think she has done an enormous quantity of excellent research in all her fields of study, and I respect her greatly for the depth and accuracy of her knowledge.

This does not mean that on occasion Cat might not be wrong on one small detail or another. In the entire history of our species, I daresay, there has not been a single scholar (or scientist, now) who has not been very seriously wrong about some point of knowledge. Nor has there even been a single adult who has gotten from birth to death without serious ethical lapses: even small children are by no means innocent creatures.

In my college years (back in the '60s) it was only the Soviets, and their American hangers-on, who used the phrase "politically correct" and who were given to turning others into "non-persons" (whose scholarship could not be cited in any context, and who could not be praised for any deed, however good) for the slightest deviation from the party line. Now that sort of thing is everywhere in the academic world, and almost everywhere in the every-day world of discourse. I despised these practices back then as an offense against the intellect, and I despise them even more after half a century, when they have infected every field of inquiry.

Also, in scholarship, lineage is bunkum. In occult matters, it is not always bunkum, since there can be actual transmission of power and gnosis. But Denise Alvarado, in the exchange cited, is speaking of scholarship more than of power or gnosis.

Yeah, steam seems to be coming out of my ears now.

comments.title: Divination systems
comments.content:
JMG (BTW, I wrote Apology above under anonymous, and thank you for your kind reply),

I’ve been looking for a system of divination that I feel comfortable with. I had been using geomancy. As helpful, and sanity-promoting as I found it, I experienced an unaccountable emotional resistance to it after several months, and dropped it. One conscious objection that I had was that the casting of the 4 geomantic figures can generate 64,000+ combinations, but the system reduces this to only 128 possible querant/quiseted/judge configurations, which bothered me. I even misplaced my geomancy book. So I'm looking for another system of divination. I’ve been looking at your Coelbren system, but I’m also taking a second look at your sacred geometry system, “Techniques for Geometric Transformation” which I bought 8 years ago, but only now find myself drawn to. It feels somehow compatible with my interest in Hermes and the Greek gods generally (shades of Pythagorus, Euclid, and Plato’s academy which required geometry…) and potentially offers a spiritual dimension my mathematical proclivities. I hope that this perceived congruence/connection with my patron and related deities will motivate better adherence to the practice. (Your book also mentions in passing the ad triangulum and ad quadratim systems of sacred geometry for practical design, which makes me curious, and which I might ask you about in due course…)

There is also Tarot, I Ching, astrology….I imagine you’d advise me to choose just one system, learn it well and practice it. The Sacred Geometry Oracle is where I think I’m headed now… Is there anything you might add or change since you wrote “Techniques for Geometric Transformation”? Down the line, is there any reason to learn and use more than one system of divination? Do different systems have different strengths? When might one choose one system versus another?

replies.title: Re: Divination systems
replies.content: I definitely recommend learning one system of divination at a time, but there's ample reason to learn one, and then learn another, and then learn a third, etc. Different oracles are better at answering different questions, and you may also find that your needs and talents change over time. I've done tarot, geomancy, Ogham, the Sacred Geometry Oracle, the Coelbren, and horary astrology, for example, and learned something from all of them.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I routinely do sequences of magical practice that keep me busy for a couple of years at a time. Right now I'm doing the Tree of Life over again, using Druidical symbolism in place of the Hermetic GD (which I used the first time through), so that I can use the practices and meditations in a future sequel to The Celtic Golden Dawn. I don't plan on using planetary material in any of the CGD sequels, because Druidry works with the archaic solar-lunar astrology rather than planetary astrology. The Tree of Life, on the other hand, is very much grist for my mill.

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
Another question on Geomancy

In checking (First and Seventh house figures) for the outcome of a lawsuit I got the following:

Seventh house passes nowhere.

First house passes to eighth and ninth which in turn make favorable aspects to fortunate Fortuna Major and Puella and also unfavorable aspects to unfortunate Carcer and Tristitia.

Do we go to Table 6-3 (page 130) of your book The Art and Practice of Geomancy and sum all the aspects to get a plus or minus forecast?

What about the "unfavorable"aspects to "fortunate" figures or "favorable" aspects to "unfavorable" figures... Do we compute them?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
Nope. You start by looking at the figures in the first and seventh houses, and consider what they themselves have to say; then you see which of them, if any, establishes one of the modes of perfection with the tenth house, which represents success in any reading about a conflict. Since the significator of the querent in the first house passes to the ninth, in conjunction with the figure in the tenth, while the significator of the opponent in the seventh doesn't pass at all, the querent wins.

You haven't mentioned which figures are in the first, seventh, and tenth, so I can't tell you whether the querent will benefit from the victory, or why the other party will lose, or any of the other things you can extract from the figures occupying those houses.

Aspects are minor factors. Aspects made by passing figures are even more minor. One of the common mistakes in geomantic divination is to pay attention to the minor factors and ignore the major ones -- which are, again, what figures are the significators of the querent, quesited, and any other primary factors (such as the opponent in the seventh), and whether they form modes of perfection among themselves. That's what matters; the minor factors simply provide little bits of commentary on the main message provided by the significators and their relationships. For example, in this case, the querent will win the lawsuit and get some of the opponent's money (the querent's significator passing to the eighth) due to making a more favorable impression with the court (the significator passing to the ninth).

Left Witness = Amissio

Judge = Carcer

replies.title: Re: Alcohol and tobacco
replies.content:
The suggestion that you should wait an hour after eating or lovemaking before practicing magic has nothing to do with penitence! It's a matter of letting your energy body settle back to normal before doing something strenuous with it, and I'd consider it a good general rule.

I've never heard of anyone who had trouble with tobacco and magic, so that's probably not an issue. (I don't use tobacco myself -- when I was growing up, my dad was a two-pack-a-day Benson & Hedges smoker, and the nausea I used to get sitting in the back seat on long car rides with the smoke piling up in fetid clouds around me has put me permanently off that particular plant -- but a lot of the older generation of operative mages smoked, some of them like chimneys, and it didn't seem to affect them at all when it came to magic.) As far as alcohol is concerned, don't meditate when you're under the influence -- it's supposed to be bad for your nervous system, and I don't encourage taking risks where that's concerned -- and if you're hung over, wait until your head and stomach have calmed down before you do your practices. Other than that, well, pay attention to your results and make such adjustments as will yield better practices.

comments.title: Statue of Liberty
comments.content:
No doubt JMG will give you a direct and concise answer to your question. I'm deeply interested in the Statue of Liberty, so I hope it's all right if I contribute some peripheral information.

That the statue was donated by French Freemasons is documented fact. You might be able to find out whether they gave Her a formal title in French at the time they made the gift. Her formal title in English is "Liberty Enlightening the World".

The physical model for the statue was an actual woman. I had assumed that her face was idealized, but when the statue was repaired in the 1980s, I read a newspaper article that included a photograph of the granddaughter of the model and she was the spitting image of Liberty.

The Romans were more given than most cultures to personifying virtues and qualities as deities, and Libertas is a good example of that. Fortuna is another. As a rule of thumb, I would say that the difference between personified forces and virtues and other classical goddesses is that the latter have myths (stories) told about them. Isis has lots of stories.

replies.content:
Anonymous, if I may, do you remember where JMG mentions "Till We Have Faces"? I haven't made it through all the comments on the current two blogs; do you remember whether you read it on Dreamwidth or Ecosophia or the Well of Galabes? I'd like to read his take on the book.

I read that novel once, rather quickly, a long time ago, and parts of it stuck with me. I thought that one of Lewis's major points was that that kind of temple paganism operated in an earlier (more primitive) state of human psychological development when most people's self-identity was not as differentiated from the group as it is now.

There was a school of thought in nineteenth and early twentieth century anthropology and psychology, when these two social sciences were just getting established as systematic fields of inquiry, that believed human cultural development progresses in predictable stages. According to this school, matriarchy was nearly universal at a prehistoric period and was all about the group. Patriarchy which supplanted it elevated the heroic individual and rationality, and this represented progress for human moral development. When C.S. Lewis was young and getting an education, this way of thinking was influential in academia.

The fuzzy memory I have of the novel is that the young woman who is the protagonist was being worshipped as a goddess in a cult pretty similar ones which still exist in parts of the Indian subcontinent (which Lewis, as an educated Brit, would have heard of) and that at the very end she either separates herself from the cult or starts thinking of the possibility that she could, and experiences that as a liberation. That is the point of the book's title--faces represent individual identities.

If that is what Lewis is driving at, he is not intending to make polytheism look good. Christianity is very much about saving souls one at a time.

I may have this entirely wrong, and even if I'm right, it does not invalidate your pleasure in the novel. The original version of the movie The Wicker Man, starring Christopher Lee, is enjoyed by many pagan witches for its fairly attractive depiction of a confident, unapologetic neopagan society, even though it's a horror movie and the novel it's based on was written by someone who thought both Christianity and neopaganism are dangerous superstitions.

replies.title: Re: Till We Have Faces
replies.content:
The idea that different stages of society reflect different levels of psychological organization in the members of the society did not die out at the end of the nineteenth century. One of its more recent incarnations was _The Origin of Consciousness in the Breakdown of the Bicameral Mind_ whose author, Julian Jaynes, proposed that modern consciousness is a recent development dating to around 3000BC, IIRC He believed that prior to this humans had a split consciousness and interpreted the communication of one brain hemisphere with another as communications from the gods. Those who retain this type of consciousness are now labeled schizophrenics. Many Pagans of my acquaintance were immensely impressed. I was less so, since he made such elementary errors as not knowing that the San Blas Indians lived on an island and that made it not a matter of wonder that their art featured whales. As far as I know, no reputable anthropologist believes that the inherent consciousness of modern non-literate peoples differs from that of modern industrialized peoples.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Till We Have Faces
replies.content:
I hold to an intermediate position, that culture and the experiences of daily life have effects on the degree to which a person experiences the self as a discrete consciousness bound in a body moving through a world filled with other discrete consciousnesses enclosed in different bodies. This is too large a subject to discuss adequately in a blog comment. I'll just give a few examples that don't depend on imagining what the thought processes of prehistoric people were like.

1. Literate people experience themselves differently than illiterates do. I am generalizing from a sample of one. I learned to read at the age of six. By that time I was capable of self-reflection. I noticed when I started to have dissociative thoughts, by which I mean mentally standing aside from myself and observing myself as if I were two people, subject and observer. That happened very soon after I was taught to read, and at the time I made a connection between this new complexity in my thought processes and that I was reading stories about characters that included the characters' thoughts. Reading taught me to think of myself as a character. Before I began to read, I was not self-aware in a divided way.

2. Our society takes individualism, alienation and distrust of others to an extreme. Despite this, some people, by report, spontaneously experience the merging of egos during lovemaking. It is, in our culture, a mostly latent capacity. People also sometimes experience it while doing athletic exercises that involve physical drill, when playing in an orchestra, etc. It seems to me (here to be honest I am speculating) that in some tribal societies which are simple enough that most adults have complete command of all the cultural knowledge, and where people spend a great deal of time in each other's company, opportunities to experience moments of ego- or consciousness-merging are likely to happen more often.

3. Several kinds of meditation aim at breaking down the sensation of separateness. Some kinds of Buddhist meditation approach this by weakening identification with the ego. Other kinds of mysticism promote stronger awareness of flow between what is inside and what is outside of the skin. I have experienced this sense of flow and connectedness pretty strongly on several occasions when I have spent time in natural environments.

replies.title: Re: Till We Have Faces
replies.content: Lewis had a very ambivalent attitude toward classical Paganism; I get the impression he was powerfully attracted by it, but basically kept on having to remind himself, "No, I'm a Christian." That was fairly common back in the day!

comments.title: Questions:
comments.content:
Hi, JMG.
I tried to do the SoP today, using the symbols and visualizations from the instructions as you reccomended, but I didn't feel very comfortable after getting used to calling on the gods themselves ("I invoke you, Hermes") instead of the elements ("I call upon the Air"). I have seen an interesting change in me since I started, and I give credit for that to the gods I've been calling. Zeus, Apollo, Hera, Aphrodite, Hermes, Hestia, Poseidon and Demeter. (I haven't tried the spirit doors yet, for I am tidying up the first four elements). So my question would be: Can I visualize them in the images I create in order to stay in contact with them? I'd say the words verbatim as in the instructions, but just picture each in the Meadow, Desert, Beach and Forest that the guide suggests. This would be the only change that I would do on this part, because I already do the Elemental Cross with the movements as instructed, but calling "Mighty Zeus", "Beautiful Appollo", "[bondadosa, meaning something like merciful or good] Hera" and "Brave Artemis", asking for their favor and protection and saying "so be it" instead of "AWEN", for I do not really know what that means... Will this affect the final result? Should I just abstain from using the greek pantheon for now? It's getting complicated, but I know very little of druid lore. :(
On another topic, I have a question regarding "druid sticks". I made mine using four of those wooden sticks that doctors use to push down on tongues, each with an elemental symbol and the dots. My question is: if I throw them and one falls outside of the surface I am using (usually beside me on the living room sofa) I check if it fell flat on the floor with a definite answer. If it didn't, like if it fell on the rug, for example, can I just throw that one again, or should I throw them all again, like dice in a casino?

Many thanks as always! I'll keep you posted on my progress :D

replies.title: Re: Questions:
replies.content:
Hi churrundo,
I also call upon the Greek gods in my SoP, but I call upon them in tandem with the elements. So for Air, I say "By the Eagle of Olympus, who flies to the Sun, and in the great name of Apollo..."

I say Awen at the appropriate times in the ritual, for several reasons:
1. I don't know an equivalent work in Greek paganism.
2. I really like the concept of Awen.
3. The word holds a central role in some of the more advanced practices in the Druid Magic Handbook.

But also, part of this path seems to be digging in and hammering out a set of practices that work for you.
-Cliff

replies.title: Re: Questions:
replies.content:
Um, it's really starting to get tiring to point out over and over again that the ritual is written the way it is for a reason, and a student who keeps on insisting on changing things before he's learned the lessons the standard version has to teach is not going to get far or accomplish much. If you aren't willing to bring an empty cup, how can you taste my tea?

As for the Druid wands, I usually just make a point of throwing them with some care, so they don't fall off the surface...

comments.title: Sacred geometry, creator-god
comments.content:
Could you explain your view of "God"? More specifically, doesn´t sacred geometry suggest that the world may have been created by a god who knows the rules of proportion, and so on? Also, there is a connection between geometry, mathematics and music. These things can only be grasped by a certain form of intellectual consciousness. "God" would therefore have these things. In your writings, I get the impression that the Divine is either an impersonal life force, or some kind of being completely indifferent to humans, but doesn´t sacred geometry suggest otherwise?

Sorry for sounding somewhat polemical, but I would really like to know...

replies.title: Re: Sacred geometry, creator-god
replies.content: I gather that you haven't read many of my writings, because I don't believe either that the divine is an impersonal life force or a being completely indifferent to humans. You might consider, if you want to know what I actually think, picking up a copy of my book A World Full of Gods, which sets out the arguments for traditional polytheism -- which is what I believe -- in quite some detail.

Happy Monday!

When you put together a new system of magic, like your Heathen Golden Dawn project, how do you know where to begin? Do you ever try a new variation of the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram, as an example, and get different results from what you intended?

Thank you for your time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I always start from an existing system of magic, so I've got a solid set of practices that simply need to be adapted to fit a different symbolism and different divine energies. I also make sure to familiarize myself in very great detail with the symbolism and energies I want to use, so I can get the results I want. Given those things, it's easy to figure out where to begin and how to get the desired results.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi,

How can someone get invited to join the Golden Dawn? There is a temple in my city, and two chief adepts will be holding a conference soon. Would it be awkward to attend this conference, despite not being a member of the order? It just seems really interesting.

Thank you again for your time.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Most Golden Dawn temples are very picky about who they admit because otherwise they get a lot of people who have no clue what they're getting into and aren't willing to put in the serious hard work needed to become a Golden Dawn initiate. If you think you might be interested, start by picking up a book on basic Golden Dawn training -- my book Learning Ritual Magic is one of these -- and doing the complete course of training in it, cover to cover, without changing or tinkering with anything. If you can do that, you probably have what it takes to become a Golden Dawn initiate -- and if you contact the temple at that point, and let them know that you've done this, you've got a better than even chance of getting an invite.

replies.title: Re: Heathen Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram
replies.content: I can do that right here. Make a plain vowel tone such as "aaaah." As you make the sound, change the way you hold your mouth and throat until the sound produces a buzzing or tingling sensation somewhere in the body. That's vibration. Do the same thing with other vowel tones until you can do it reliably, and then do it with the names you intone.

comments.title: Violence elsewhere
comments.content:
Hello
I've been doing my three elements of practice for few months now. Previously I've done Eastern style meditation, which became unmanageable with very disturbing imagery. Your advice on banishing and prayer was solid, thank you.
So since then I am learning to practice discursive meditation, which I consider harder work, maybe since it's unusual and new to me but I found the practice of opening the mind and emptying of thoughts easier - now I can see how naive that actually sounds.
Sometimes when I am relaxed I slip and let my mind be unguarded and again and consistently very frightening entities stand right in front of me. I learned in this situation to quickly ask for angel protection and "close up" my mind. It seems too work. I want to continue with my daily practice.

In this context I just finished Barbara Ehrenreich's book Living with a wild God, where she describes her dissociations and very violent visions and cites other cases where people also have rather scary and violent visions and manifestations.
Are the other worlds violent? Much more so then we are willing to believe? Did you have experience with that kind of violence?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Violence elsewhere
replies.content: The traditional occult explanation is that the lower half or so of the astral plane -- the plane of concrete consciousness, which is where we experience things like dreams and visual imagery -- fills up over time with toxic images and influences. (You can think of this as the magical equivalent of air pollution!) Eventually that burden of toxic energies discharges onto the material plane in the form of disasters. Right now, the lower astral is a very rough place, full of nasty energies which express themselves as violent and hateful imagery. This is why daily banishing rituals are so essential in magical practice right now.

comments.title: opal
comments.content:
Greetings JMG.

I received my copy of the encyclopedia of natural magic a few days ago (as well as Green Wizardry, which is destined to be a birthday gift for a friend), thanks to the Aussie business I mentioned on the other blog, Booktopia. Very excited... and btw happy birthday.

Flipping through the encyclopedia I came instantly upon the entry for opal. This is notable, as I am a lapidarist and had published an article on opal in the previous edition of my club newsletter. As you may know, Australia is richly endowed with opals and not a little with opal madness. A disproportionate number of lapidary club members are afflicted by what I perceive as quite a sinister monomania for opal. Its ill repute used to surprise me till I heard stories from the nasty world of the mining towns. 

If I may, I would like to exploit this synchronicity and make a few tiny observations. Black opal is indeed rare and is the most expensive variety. To clarify, black opal has play of colour as seen in other precious opals; however, the background tones are dark. The general association of opal with bad luck, while well known, seems to be minor in practice and has little negative effect on the amazing attraction these stones wield. For they can be captivatingly, magically beautiful. They command enormous sums and a strong export industry (perhaps very black in itself, so to speak) exists in this country. Some more information and pictures, for the curious, may be found at https://www.mindat.org/min-3004.html and http://www.ga.gov.au/education/classroom-resources/minerals-energy/australian-mineral-facts/opal

Regards

kallianeira
